epigraph.text:
He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has 
put eternity into man's heart.

content.text:
Consider a particle moving along a curved path in the plane. At each instant
$t$, the particle occupies a position $(x,y)$. Both $x$ and $y$ depend on
time: $x = f(t)$ and $y = g(t)$. This is the fundamental idea of
**parametric equations**—describing a curve not as $y$ in terms of $x$,
but as both coordinates in terms of a third variable, the *parameter*.

content.title: Parametric Equations
content.content:
A **parametric curve** in the plane is a set
of points $(x,y)$ defined by
\[
 x = f(t), \qquad y = g(t),
\]
where $f$ and $g$ are continuous functions on some interval $I$. The variable
$t$ is called the **parameter**. As $t$ varies over
$I$, the point $(x,y)$ traces a curve called the **trajectory** or
**parametric path**.

content.text:
The parameter $t$ often represents time, but it need not. It could represent
angle, arc length, or any other quantity that varies continuously. What
matters is that $t$ provides an ordering—a sense of direction along the
curve.

content.title: A line segment
content.problem: Describe the line segment from $(1, 2)$ to $(4, 6)$ parametrically.
content.solution:
We seek $x = f(t)$ and $y = g(t)$ such that at $t = 0$ we are at $(1,2)$
and at $t = 1$ we are at $(4,6)$. Linear interpolation gives:
\[
 x = 1 + 3t, \qquad y = 2 + 4t, \qquad 0 \le t \le 1.
\]
At $t = 0$: $(x,y) = (1,2)$. At $t = 1$: $(x,y) = (4,6)$. As $t$ increases
from $0$ to $1$, the point moves from start to end.

content.caption: A line segment from $(1,2)$ to $(4,6)$ traced as $t$ increases from 0 to 1
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing a line segment from point (1,2) to point (4,6). An arrow along the segment indicates the direction of increasing parameter t, with the starting point labeled at t=0 and ending point at t=1.

content.title: The unit circle
content.problem: Describe the unit circle parametrically.
content.solution:
The most natural parametrization uses angle:
\[
 x = \cos t, \qquad y = \sin t, \qquad 0 \le t \le 2\pi.
\]
As $t$ increases from $0$ to $2\pi$, the point travels counterclockwise
around the circle, starting and ending at $(1,0)$.

**Alternative:** To trace the circle twice, use $0 \le t \le 4\pi$.
To trace clockwise, use $x = \cos(-t) = \cos t$, $y = \sin(-t) = -\sin t$.
The same geometric circle can have many parametrizations.

content.caption: The unit circle $x = \cos t$, $y = \sin t$ with counterclockwise orientation
content.alt: The unit circle centered at the origin with radius 1. Key points are marked at (1,0), (0,1), (-1,0), and (0,-1) corresponding to t=0, π/2, π, and 3π/2 respectively. An arrow indicates counterclockwise traversal as the parameter t increases from 0 to 2π.

content.title: An ellipse
content.problem: Parametrize the ellipse $\dfrac{x^2}{9} + \dfrac{y^2}{4} = 1$.

content.caption: An ellipse with semi-major axis $a=3$ and semi-minor axis $b=2$
content.alt: An ellipse centered at the origin with semi-major axis a=3 along the x-axis and semi-minor axis b=2 along the y-axis. The vertices at (3,0), (-3,0), (0,2), and (0,-2) are marked. An arrow indicates counterclockwise traversal as the parameter t increases from 0 to 2π.

content.title: A hyperbola
content.problem: Parametrize the right branch of the hyperbola $\dfrac{x^2}{4} - \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$.
content.solution:
We use hyperbolic functions:
\[
 x = 2\cosh t, \qquad y = 3\sinh t, \qquad -\infty < t < \infty.
\]
Check: $\dfrac{(2\cosh t)^2}{4} - \dfrac{(3\sinh t)^2}{9} = \cosh^2 t - \sinh^2 t = 1$.\;

Since $\cosh t \ge 1$ for all $t$, we have $x \ge 2$, which gives only the
right branch. As $t$ increases from $-\infty$ to $\infty$, the curve is
traced from bottom to top.

Alternatively, we can use trigonometric parametrization:
\[
 x = 2\sec\theta, \qquad y = 3\tan\theta, \qquad -\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \frac{\pi}{2}.
\]

content.title: A semicubical parabola
content.problem: Sketch the curve $x = t^2$, $y = t^3$ and identify it.
content.solution:
From $x = t^2$, we have $t = \pm\sqrt{x}$. Substituting into $y = t^3$:
\[
 y = (\pm\sqrt{x})^3 = \pm x^{3/2},
\]
so $y^2 = x^3$. This is called a **semicubical parabola** or 
**Neil's parabola**.

The curve has a *cusp* at the origin—a point where the curve comes
to a sharp point and reverses direction. This occurs at $t = 0$.

content.caption: The semicubical parabola $y^2 = x^3$
content.alt: A semicubical parabola (Neil's parabola) with equation y² = x³. The curve has a cusp at the origin where both branches meet sharply. The upper branch curves upward into the first quadrant while the lower branch curves downward into the fourth quadrant, both extending to the right as x increases.

content.text: To sketch a parametric curve by hand:

items: Choose several values of $t$ in the given interval.
Plot the points and connect them in order of increasing $t$.
Indicate the direction of motion with arrows.

content.title: Sketching a parabolic path
content.problem: Sketch the curve $x = t^2$, $y = t$ for $-2 \le t \le 2$.
content.solution:
| $t$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
| $x$ | $4$ | $1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $4$ |
| $y$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |

The points are $(4,-2)$, $(1,-1)$, $(0,0)$, $(1,1)$, $(4,2)$. Notice that
$x = y^2$, so this is a *parabola opening to the right*. The direction
of motion is from bottom to top (as $t$ increases, $y = t$ increases).

content.caption: A parabola opening to the right: $x = y^2$
content.alt: A parabola opening to the right with equation x = y². The vertex is at the origin. Points are plotted at (4,-2), (1,-1), (0,0), (1,1), and (4,2). An arrow indicates the direction of motion from bottom to top as the parameter t increases from -2 to 2.

content.title: A cycloid
content.problem:
A **cycloid** is the path traced by a point on the rim
of a circle of radius $r$ rolling along a line. Its parametric equations are:
\[
 x = r(t - \sin t), \qquad y = r(1 - \cos t).
\]
content.solution:
For $r = 1$:

| $t$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ | $\pi$ | $3\pi/2$ | $2\pi$ |
| $x$ | $0$ | $\pi/2 - 1$ | $\pi$ | $3\pi/2 + 1$ | $2\pi$ |
| $y$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $1$ | $0$ |

The curve forms a series of arches, each spanning $2\pi r$ horizontally and
reaching height $2r$.

The cycloid has remarkable physical properties: it is both the 
*brachistochrone* (curve of fastest descent) and the *tautochrone* 
(curve of equal-time descent).

content.caption: One arch of a cycloid generated by a rolling circle of radius 1
content.alt: One complete arch of a cycloid curve. The curve starts at the origin, rises to a maximum height of 2 at x = π, then descends back to touch the x-axis at x = 2π. A small circle is shown rolling along the x-axis to illustrate how the curve is generated, with a point on the rim tracing the cycloid path.

content.title: The Brachistochrone Problem
content.content:
In 1696, Johann Bernoulli challenged mathematicians to find the curve of
fastest descent—the path along which a bead sliding under gravity from $A$
to $B$ arrives in minimum time. Newton, Leibniz, L'H\^opital, and Jakob
Bernoulli all solved it: the answer is the cycloid. This problem launched
the calculus of variations and demonstrated that nature's optimal paths are
often surprising curves, not straight lines.

Bernoulli famously recognized Newton's anonymous solution, saying "I recognize
the lion by his claw" (*tanquam ex ungue leonem*).

content.title: The witch of Agnesi
content.problem:
The **witch of Agnesi** is the curve with 
parametric equations:
\[
 x = 2a\tan t, \qquad y = 2a\cos^2 t, \qquad -\frac{\pi}{2} < t < \frac{\pi}{2}.
\]
content.solution:
To eliminate the parameter, note that $\sec^2 t = 1 + \tan^2 t = 1 + \dfrac{x^2}{4a^2}$.
Since $y = 2a\cos^2 t = \dfrac{2a}{\sec^2 t}$:
\[
 y = \frac{2a}{1 + \frac{x^2}{4a^2}} = \frac{8a^3}{4a^2 + x^2}.
\]

For $a = 1$: $y = \dfrac{8}{4 + x^2}$. This is a bell-shaped curve with 
maximum at $(0, 2)$, asymptotic to the $x$-axis.

content.caption: The witch of Agnesi: $y = \dfrac{8}{4 + x^2}$
content.alt: The witch of Agnesi curve, a smooth bell-shaped curve symmetric about the y-axis. The curve has its maximum at the point (0, 2) and approaches the x-axis asymptotically as x approaches positive or negative infinity. The curve resembles a probability distribution but is not Gaussian.

content.title: Maria Gaetana Agnesi
content.content:
Maria Gaetana Agnesi (1718–1799) was an Italian mathematician who wrote one of 
the first calculus textbooks, *Instituzioni analitiche* (1748). She studied
the curve $y = \dfrac{a^3}{x^2 + a^2}$, which she called *la versiera* 
(from Latin *vertere*, "to turn"). The British mathematician John Colson
mistranslated this as *l'avversiera* ("the witch"), giving us the 
colorful but unfortunate name "witch of Agnesi."

content.title: Lissajous figures
content.problem:
**Lissajous figures** have parametric equations:
\[
 x = A\sin(at + \delta), \qquad y = B\sin(bt).
\]
Sketch the curve when $A = B = 1$, $a = 3$, $b = 2$, and $\delta = 0$.
content.solution:
With $x = \sin(3t)$ and $y = \sin(2t)$:

The ratio $a:b$ determines the shape. When $a:b$ is a ratio of integers, the
curve closes; otherwise, it fills a rectangle densely. Lissajous figures
appear on oscilloscopes when comparing two sinusoidal signals.

content.caption: A Lissajous figure with frequency ratio $a:b = 3:2$
content.alt: A Lissajous figure showing the curve x = sin(3t), y = sin(2t). The closed curve has a figure-eight-like appearance with three horizontal lobes and two vertical lobes, creating an intricate pattern that fits within a square from -1 to 1 on both axes. The curve crosses itself at several interior points.

content.text:
Sometimes we want to convert parametric equations to a Cartesian equation by
**eliminating the parameter**. This reveals the shape of the curve but
loses information about direction and speed.

content.title: Eliminating the Parameter
content.content:
To convert $x = f(t)$, $y = g(t)$ to a Cartesian equation:

1. Solve one equation for $t$ (if possible).

2. Substitute into the other equation.

3. Alternatively, use identities (especially $\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t = 1$
 or $\cosh^2 t - \sinh^2 t = 1$).

content.title: Eliminating from a line
content.problem: Eliminate the parameter from $x = 1 + 3t$, $y = 2 + 4t$.
content.solution:
From the first equation: $t = \frac{x-1}{3}$. Substitute into the second:
\[
 y = 2 + 4\cdot\frac{x-1}{3} = 2 + \frac{4x - 4}{3} = \frac{4x + 2}{3}.
\]
So $y = \frac{4}{3}x + \frac{2}{3}$, or $4x - 3y + 2 = 0$—a line, as expected.

content.title: Eliminating using a trig identity
content.problem: Eliminate the parameter from $x = 3\cos t$, $y = 2\sin t$.
content.solution:
Solve for the trig functions: $\cos t = x/3$, $\sin t = y/2$. Apply the
Pythagorean identity:
\[
 \cos^2 t + \sin^2 t = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 \frac{x^2}{9} + \frac{y^2}{4} = 1.
\]
This is the standard equation of an ellipse with semi-major axis $3$ and
semi-minor axis $2$.

content.title: Eliminating with hyperbolic identity
content.problem: Eliminate the parameter from $x = 2\cosh t$, $y = 3\sinh t$.
content.solution:
Solve: $\cosh t = x/2$, $\sinh t = y/3$. Apply the hyperbolic identity:
\[
 \cosh^2 t - \sinh^2 t = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 \frac{x^2}{4} - \frac{y^2}{9} = 1.
\]
This is a hyperbola. Note the minus sign, corresponding to the minus in the
identity.

content.title: A more challenging elimination
content.problem: Eliminate the parameter from $x = \cos^3 t$, $y = \sin^3 t$.
content.solution:
We have $\cos t = x^{1/3}$ and $\sin t = y^{1/3}$. Applying the Pythagorean
identity:
\[
 (x^{1/3})^2 + (y^{1/3})^2 = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 x^{2/3} + y^{2/3} = 1.
\]
This curve is called an **astroid**—a four-cusped
hypocycloid.

content.caption: An astroid: $x^{2/3} + y^{2/3} = 1$
content.alt: An astroid (four-cusped hypocycloid) with equation x^(2/3) + y^(2/3) = 1. The curve has four sharp cusps located at (1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), and (0,-1), with smooth concave arcs connecting adjacent cusps. The overall shape resembles a four-pointed star inscribed in a square.

content.title: Elimination may lose information
content.content:
The Cartesian equation $\dfrac{x^2}{9} + \dfrac{y^2}{4} = 1$ describes the
entire ellipse, but it says nothing about:

- Which direction the curve is traced

- Where the tracing starts and ends

- How fast different portions are traversed
The parametric form $x = 3\cos t$, $y = 2\sin t$ for $0 \le t \le 2\pi$
provides all this information: counterclockwise, starting at $(3,0)$, one
complete revolution.

content.text:
Given a parametric curve $x = f(t)$, $y = g(t)$, how do we find the slope
$dy/dx$ without first eliminating the parameter?

By the chain rule, if $x = f(t)$ and $y = g(t)$:
\[
 \deriv{y}{t} = \deriv{y}{x}\cdot\deriv{x}{t}.
\]
Solving for $dy/dx$:

content.title: Slope of a Parametric Curve
content.content:
If $x = f(t)$ and $y = g(t)$ are differentiable and $dx/dt \ne 0$, then
\[
 \deriv{y}{x} = \frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt} = \frac{g'(t)}{f'(t)}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Slope of a cycloid
content.problem: Find the slope of the cycloid $x = t - \sin t$, $y = 1 - \cos t$ at $t = \pi/2$.
content.solution:
Compute the derivatives:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = 1 - \cos t, \qquad \deriv{y}{t} = \sin t.
\]
At $t = \pi/2$:
\[
 \deriv{y}{x} = \frac{\sin(\pi/2)}{1 - \cos(\pi/2)} = \frac{1}{1 - 0} = 1.
\]
The tangent line has slope $1$ at this point.

content.title: Slope of an ellipse at a point
content.problem:
Find the slope of the ellipse $x = 4\cos t$, $y = 3\sin t$ at the point
where $t = \pi/3$.
content.solution:
We have:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = -4\sin t, \qquad \deriv{y}{t} = 3\cos t.
\]
At $t = \pi/3$:
\[
 \deriv{y}{x} = \frac{3\cos(\pi/3)}{-4\sin(\pi/3)} 
 = \frac{3 \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{-4 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}
 = \frac{3/2}{-2\sqrt{3}} = -\frac{3}{4\sqrt{3}} = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}.
\]
The point is $(4\cos(\pi/3), 3\sin(\pi/3)) = (2, \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2})$.

content.title: Horizontal and vertical tangents
content.problem:
Find all points where the curve $x = t^3 - 3t$, $y = t^2 - 4$ has horizontal
or vertical tangent lines.
content.solution:
Compute: $dx/dt = 3t^2 - 3 = 3(t^2 - 1)$ and $dy/dt = 2t$.

**Horizontal tangents** occur where $dy/dt = 0$ and $dx/dt \ne 0$:
\[
 2t = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 0.
\]
At $t = 0$: $(x,y) = (0,-4)$ and $dx/dt = -3 \ne 0$.\;

**Vertical tangents** occur where $dx/dt = 0$ and $dy/dt \ne 0$:
\[
 3(t^2 - 1) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = \pm 1.
\]
At $t = 1$: $(x,y) = (-2, -3)$ and $dy/dt = 2 \ne 0$.\; \\
At $t = -1$: $(x,y) = (2, -3)$ and $dy/dt = -2 \ne 0$.\;

content.caption: Horizontal and vertical tangent points on $x = t^3 - 3t$, $y = t^2 - 4$
content.alt: A parametric curve x = t³ - 3t, y = t² - 4 with marked special points. The point (0, -4) is labeled as having a horizontal tangent. The points (-2, -3) and (2, -3) are labeled as having vertical tangents. Tangent lines are drawn at each of these points to illustrate the horizontal and vertical orientations.

content.title: Tangent to an astroid
content.problem:
Find the equation of the tangent line to the astroid $x = \cos^3 t$, 
$y = \sin^3 t$ at $t = \pi/4$.
content.solution:
Compute the derivatives:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = 3\cos^2 t \cdot (-\sin t) = -3\cos^2 t\sin t,
\]
\[
 \deriv{y}{t} = 3\sin^2 t \cdot \cos t = 3\sin^2 t\cos t.
\]
The slope is:
\[
 \deriv{y}{x} = \frac{3\sin^2 t\cos t}{-3\cos^2 t\sin t} = -\frac{\sin t}{\cos t} = -\tan t.
\]
At $t = \pi/4$: slope $= -\tan(\pi/4) = -1$.

The point is $(\cos^3(\pi/4), \sin^3(\pi/4)) = \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)$.

The tangent line is:
\[
 y - \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} = -1\left(x - \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = -x + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}.
\]

content.text:
To find $d^2y/dx^2$ for concavity analysis, we differentiate $dy/dx$ with
respect to $x$. Using the chain rule again:

content.title: Second Derivative of Parametric Curves
content.content:
If $x = f(t)$ and $y = g(t)$ are twice differentiable and $dx/dt \ne 0$, then
\[
 \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\deriv{y}{x}\right)
 = \frac{\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)}{\dfrac{dx}{dt}}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Concavity of a parametric curve
content.problem: Determine the concavity of $x = t^2$, $y = t^3$ for $t > 0$.
content.solution:
First derivative:
\[
 \deriv{y}{x} = \frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt} = \frac{3t^2}{2t} = \frac{3t}{2}.
\]
Second derivative:
\[
 \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{3t}{2}\right)}{\dfrac{dx}{dt}}
 = \frac{3/2}{2t} = \frac{3}{4t}.
\]
For $t > 0$, we have $d^2y/dx^2 > 0$, so the curve is **concave up**.

content.title: Concavity of a cycloid
content.problem:
Analyze the concavity of the cycloid $x = t - \sin t$, $y = 1 - \cos t$ for
$0 < t < 2\pi$.
content.solution:
From earlier: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{\sin t}{1 - \cos t}$.

We need $\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{\sin t}{1 - \cos t}\right)$. Using the quotient rule:
\[
 \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\sin t}{1 - \cos t}\right) 
 = \frac{\cos t(1 - \cos t) - \sin t \cdot \sin t}{(1 - \cos t)^2}
 = \frac{\cos t - \cos^2 t - \sin^2 t}{(1 - \cos t)^2}
 = \frac{\cos t - 1}{(1 - \cos t)^2}
 = \frac{-1}{1 - \cos t}.
\]

Therefore:
\[
 \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{-1/(1-\cos t)}{1 - \cos t} = \frac{-1}{(1-\cos t)^2} < 0.
\]

For $0 < t < 2\pi$, we have $1 - \cos t > 0$, so $d^2y/dx^2 < 0$. The cycloid
is **concave down** throughout its arch.

content.title: Inflection points
content.problem: Find the inflection points of $x = t - t^3$, $y = t^2$.
content.solution:
First derivative:
\[
 \deriv{y}{x} = \frac{2t}{1 - 3t^2}.
\]

To find $d^2y/dx^2$, differentiate with respect to $t$:
\[
 \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{2t}{1 - 3t^2}\right) 
 = \frac{2(1-3t^2) - 2t(-6t)}{(1-3t^2)^2}
 = \frac{2 - 6t^2 + 12t^2}{(1-3t^2)^2}
 = \frac{2 + 6t^2}{(1-3t^2)^2}.
\]

Then:
\[
 \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{(2 + 6t^2)/(1-3t^2)^2}{1 - 3t^2} 
 = \frac{2 + 6t^2}{(1-3t^2)^3}.
\]

The second derivative changes sign when $(1 - 3t^2)^3$ changes sign, which
occurs at $t = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$. At these values:
\[
 (x, y) = \left(\pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} - \frac{1}{3\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{3}\right)
 = \left(\pm\frac{2}{3\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{3}\right)
 = \left(\pm\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{9}, \frac{1}{3}\right).
\]
These are the inflection points.

content.text:
Recall that the arc length element is $ds = \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2}$. For 
parametric curves, $dx = f'(t)\,dt$ and $dy = g'(t)\,dt$, so:

content.title: Arc Length of a Parametric Curve
content.content:
If $x = f(t)$ and $y = g(t)$ have continuous derivatives on $[\alpha, \beta]$,
and the curve is traversed exactly once as $t$ goes from $\alpha$ to $\beta$,
then the arc length is
\[
 L = \defint{\alpha}{\beta}\sqrt{\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2
 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2}\dt.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Arc length of an ellipse (setup)
content.problem:
Set up the integral for the arc length of the ellipse $x = 3\cos t$,
$y = 2\sin t$ for $0 \le t \le 2\pi$.
content.solution:
We have $dx/dt = -3\sin t$ and $dy/dt = 2\cos t$, so
\[
 \left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2
 = 9\sin^2 t + 4\cos^2 t = 4 + 5\sin^2 t.
\]
Therefore:
\[
 L = \defint{0}{2\pi}\sqrt{4 + 5\sin^2 t}\,dt.
\]
This is an *elliptic integral*—it has no elementary closed form.
The perimeter of an ellipse cannot be expressed using elementary functions!
Numerical methods give $L \approx 15.87$.

content.title: Arc length of a circle
content.problem: Verify the circumference formula for a circle of radius $r$.
content.solution:
Parametrize: $x = r\cos t$, $y = r\sin t$ for $0 \le t \le 2\pi$. Then
\[
 \left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2
 = r^2\sin^2 t + r^2\cos^2 t = r^2.
\]
So:
\[
 L = \defint{0}{2\pi}\sqrt{r^2}\,dt = \defint{0}{2\pi}r\,dt = 2\pi r.\;\checkmark
\]

content.title: Arc length of a cycloid arch
content.problem:
Find the length of one arch of the cycloid $x = t - \sin t$, $y = 1 - \cos t$
for $0 \le t \le 2\pi$.
content.solution:
Compute:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = 1 - \cos t, \qquad \deriv{y}{t} = \sin t.
\]
Then:
$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2
 &= (1 - \cos t)^2 + \sin^2 t \\
 &= 1 - 2\cos t + \cos^2 t + \sin^2 t \\
 &= 2 - 2\cos t = 2(1 - \cos t).
\end{aligned}$$
Using the identity $1 - \cos t = 2\sin^2(t/2)$:
\[
 \sqrt{2(1 - \cos t)} = \sqrt{4\sin^2(t/2)} = 2|\sin(t/2)| = 2\sin(t/2)
\]
(since $0 \le t/2 \le \pi$ implies $\sin(t/2) \ge 0$).

Therefore:
\[
 L = \defint{0}{2\pi}2\sin(t/2)\,dt
 = 2\cdot\Bigl[-2\cos(t/2)\Bigr]_0^{2\pi}
 = -4[\cos\pi - \cos 0] = -4(-1 - 1) = 8.
\]
One arch of a cycloid (generated by a circle of radius $1$) has length $8$—
exactly $8$ times the radius!

content.title: Arc length of an astroid
content.problem:
Find the arc length of the astroid $x = \cos^3 t$, $y = \sin^3 t$ for
$0 \le t \le 2\pi$.
content.solution:
Compute:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = -3\cos^2 t\sin t, \qquad \deriv{y}{t} = 3\sin^2 t\cos t.
\]
Then:
$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2
 &= 9\cos^4 t\sin^2 t + 9\sin^4 t\cos^2 t \\
 &= 9\cos^2 t\sin^2 t(\cos^2 t + \sin^2 t) \\
 &= 9\cos^2 t\sin^2 t = \frac{9}{4}\sin^2(2t).
\end{aligned}$$

So:
\[
 \sqrt{\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2} 
 = \frac{3}{2}|\sin(2t)|.
\]

By symmetry, we can compute the arc length in the first quadrant 
($0 \le t \le \pi/2$) and multiply by $4$:
\[
 L = 4 \cdot \defint{0}{\pi/2}\frac{3}{2}\sin(2t)\,dt
 = 6\left[-\frac{\cos(2t)}{2}\right]_0^{\pi/2}
 = -3[\cos\pi - \cos 0] = -3(-1-1) = 6.
\]
The total arc length of the astroid is $6$.

content.title: Arc length with exponentials
content.problem: Find the arc length of $x = e^t\cos t$, $y = e^t\sin t$ from $t = 0$ to $t = \pi$.
content.solution:
This is a **logarithmic spiral** in parametric form. Compute:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = e^t\cos t - e^t\sin t = e^t(\cos t - \sin t),
\]
\[
 \deriv{y}{t} = e^t\sin t + e^t\cos t = e^t(\sin t + \cos t).
\]
Then:
$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2
 &= e^{2t}[(\cos t - \sin t)^2 + (\sin t + \cos t)^2] \\
 &= e^{2t}[\cos^2 t - 2\sin t\cos t + \sin^2 t + \sin^2 t + 2\sin t\cos t + \cos^2 t] \\
 &= e^{2t}[2\cos^2 t + 2\sin^2 t] = 2e^{2t}.
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
\[
 L = \defint{0}{\pi}\sqrt{2e^{2t}}\,dt = \sqrt{2}\defint{0}{\pi}e^t\,dt
 = \sqrt{2}[e^t]_0^{\pi} = \sqrt{2}(e^{\pi} - 1) \approx 31.3.
\]

content.text:
When a parametric curve is revolved about an axis, it generates a surface.
Combining the parametric arc length element with the surface area formula:

content.title: Surface Area of Revolution (Parametric)
content.content:
If a smooth parametric curve $x = f(t)$, $y = g(t)$ for $\alpha \le t \le \beta$
is revolved about the $x$-axis (with $y \ge 0$), the surface area is
\[
 S = 2\pi\defint{\alpha}{\beta} y(t)\sqrt{\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2
 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2}\dt.
\]
For revolution about the $y$-axis (with $x \ge 0$):
\[
 S = 2\pi\defint{\alpha}{\beta} x(t)\sqrt{\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2
 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2}\dt.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Surface area of a torus
content.problem:
A **torus** is generated by revolving a circle of radius $r$
centered at $(R, 0)$ about the $y$-axis (where $R > r$). Find its surface area.
content.solution:
Parametrize the circle as $x = R + r\cos t$, $y = r\sin t$ for $0 \le t \le 2\pi$.
Then:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = -r\sin t, \qquad \deriv{y}{t} = r\cos t,
\]
so
\[
 \sqrt{\left(\deriv{x}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\deriv{y}{t}\right)^2}
 = \sqrt{r^2\sin^2 t + r^2\cos^2 t} = r.
\]
The surface area (revolving about the $y$-axis) is:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 2\pi\defint{0}{2\pi}(R + r\cos t)\cdot r\,dt \\
 &= 2\pi r\defint{0}{2\pi}(R + r\cos t)\,dt \\
 &= 2\pi r\Bigl[Rt + r\sin t\Bigr]_0^{2\pi} \\
 &= 2\pi r(2\pi R) = 4\pi^2 Rr.
\end{aligned}$$
The surface area of a torus is $4\pi^2 Rr$.

content.caption: Cross-section of a torus showing the generating circle
content.alt: A cross-sectional view of a torus in the xy-plane. A circle of radius r is centered at point (R, 0) on the positive x-axis, where R > r. The y-axis represents the axis of revolution. Labels indicate the major radius R (from origin to circle center) and minor radius r (radius of the generating circle).

content.title: Surface of revolution: sphere check
content.problem: Verify the surface area of a sphere by revolving a semicircle parametrically.
content.solution:
Parametrize the upper semicircle: $x = R\cos t$, $y = R\sin t$ for
$0 \le t \le \pi$. Then $dx/dt = -R\sin t$, $dy/dt = R\cos t$, and
\[
 \sqrt{(dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2} = R.
\]
Revolving about the $x$-axis:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 2\pi\defint{0}{\pi}R\sin t \cdot R\,dt = 2\pi R^2\defint{0}{\pi}\sin t\,dt \\
 &= 2\pi R^2[-\cos t]_0^{\pi} = 2\pi R^2(1 + 1) = 4\pi R^2.\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Surface area of a paraboloid
content.problem:
Find the surface area generated by revolving $x = t^2$, $y = t$ for
$0 \le t \le 2$ about the $x$-axis.
content.solution:
We have $dx/dt = 2t$ and $dy/dt = 1$, so
\[
 \sqrt{(dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2} = \sqrt{4t^2 + 1}.
\]

The surface area is:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 2\pi\defint{0}{2} t\sqrt{4t^2 + 1}\,dt.
\end{aligned}$$

Let $u = 4t^2 + 1$, then $du = 8t\,dt$, so $t\,dt = du/8$. When $t = 0$,
$u = 1$; when $t = 2$, $u = 17$.
$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{8}\defint{1}{17}\sqrt{u}\,du 
 = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[\frac{2u^{3/2}}{3}\right]_1^{17} \\
 &= \frac{\pi}{6}(17^{3/2} - 1) = \frac{\pi}{6}(17\sqrt{17} - 1) \approx 36.2.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Gabriel's horn revisited
content.problem:
The curve $y = 1/x$ for $x \ge 1$ can be parametrized as $x = t$, $y = 1/t$
for $t \ge 1$. Set up the integral for the surface area when revolved about
the $x$-axis.
content.solution:
We have $dx/dt = 1$ and $dy/dt = -1/t^2$, so
\[
 \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{t^4}} = \frac{\sqrt{t^4 + 1}}{t^2}.
\]

The surface area is:
\[
 S = 2\pi\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{t}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{t^4+1}}{t^2}\,dt
 = 2\pi\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{\sqrt{t^4+1}}{t^3}\,dt.
\]

This integral diverges (as shown in Chapter 7), leading to the paradox that
Gabriel's horn has finite volume but infinite surface area.

content.title: Projectile Motion
content.content:
Galileo Galilei (1564–1642) discovered that the path of a projectile (neglecting
air resistance) is a parabola. He described it parametrically: horizontal motion
at constant velocity and vertical motion under constant gravitational acceleration.
This was one of the first applications of parametric thinking in physics.

content.title: Projectile motion
content.problem:
A ball is thrown from ground level at angle $\theta = 45°$ with initial speed
$v_0 = 30$ m/s. Find parametric equations for its path and determine:
(a) when it lands, (b) how far it travels, (c) its maximum height.
content.solution:
The parametric equations for projectile motion are:
\[
 x = (v_0\cos\theta)t, \qquad y = (v_0\sin\theta)t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2,
\]
where $g \approx 9.8$ m/s$^2$.

With $v_0 = 30$ and $\theta = 45°$:
\[
 x = 30\cdot\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\,t = 15\sqrt{2}\,t, \qquad
 y = 15\sqrt{2}\,t - 4.9t^2.
\]

**(a) Landing time:** Set $y = 0$:
\[
 t(15\sqrt{2} - 4.9t) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 0 \text{ or } 
 t = \frac{15\sqrt{2}}{4.9} \approx 4.33\text{ s}.
\]

**(b) Horizontal range:**
\[
 x = 15\sqrt{2} \cdot 4.33 \approx 91.8\text{ m}.
\]

**(c) Maximum height:** Set $dy/dt = 0$:
\[
 15\sqrt{2} - 9.8t = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = \frac{15\sqrt{2}}{9.8} \approx 2.16\text{ s}.
\]
Then:
\[
 y_{\max} = 15\sqrt{2}(2.16) - 4.9(2.16)^2 \approx 22.9\text{ m}.
\]

content.title: Bezier curves
content.problem:
A **quadratic B\'ezier curve** with control points
$P_0 = (0,0)$, $P_1 = (1,2)$, $P_2 = (3,0)$ has parametric equations:
\[
 (x,y) = (1-t)^2 P_0 + 2(1-t)t P_1 + t^2 P_2, \qquad 0 \le t \le 1.
\]
Find the explicit equations and sketch the curve.
content.solution:
Expanding:
$$\begin{aligned}
x &= (1-t)^2(0) + 2(1-t)t(1) + t^2(3) = 2t - 2t^2 + 3t^2 = 2t + t^2, \\
 y &= (1-t)^2(0) + 2(1-t)t(2) + t^2(0) = 4t - 4t^2 = 4t(1-t).
\end{aligned}$$

B\'ezier curves are fundamental in computer graphics and font design. The
curve starts at $P_0$, ends at $P_2$, and is "pulled toward" $P_1$.

content.caption: A quadratic Bézier curve with control points $P_0$, $P_1$, $P_2$
content.alt: A quadratic Bézier curve starting at control point P₀ = (0,0), ending at P₂ = (3,0), and curving toward P₁ = (1,2). The control polygon (dashed lines connecting P₀ to P₁ to P₂) is shown to illustrate how the curve is 'pulled' toward P₁. The smooth parabolic curve lies entirely within the control polygon.

content.title: Pursuit curves
content.problem:
A dog at the origin sees its owner at $(1, 0)$ and runs directly toward the
owner at speed $v$. If the owner walks along the positive $y$-axis at speed
$u$, describe the dog's path parametrically.
content.solution:
This is a classic **pursuit curve** problem. Let the dog's position be
$(x(t), y(t))$ and the owner's position be $(0, ut)$.

The dog always runs toward the owner, so its velocity vector points from
$(x, y)$ toward $(0, ut)$. The direction is:
\[
 \frac{(0 - x, ut - y)}{\sqrt{x^2 + (ut-y)^2}}.
\]
Since the dog's speed is $v$:
\[
 \deriv{x}{t} = \frac{-vx}{\sqrt{x^2 + (ut-y)^2}}, \qquad
 \deriv{y}{t} = \frac{v(ut-y)}{\sqrt{x^2 + (ut-y)^2}}.
\]

This system of differential equations has an elegant solution when $v = u$:
the dog catches the owner at $y = 1/2$. When $v < u$, the dog never catches
up; when $v > u$, it catches the owner in finite time.

marginNotes.text:
To everything there is a season, and a time
to every purpose under the heaven.

You discern my going out and my lying down; you
are familiar with all my ways.

It is the glory of God to conceal a matter;
to search out a matter is the glory of kings.

Oh, the depth of the riches of the wisdom and
knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and his paths beyond tracing
out!

He determines the number of the stars and calls
them each by name.

O Lord, how manifold are your works! In wisdom
you have made them all; the earth is full of your creatures.

By wisdom the Lord laid the earth's foundations,
by understanding he set the heavens in place.

marginNotes.author: Henri Poincar\'e (1854--1912)
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the art of giving
the same name to different things.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton (1643--1727)
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further it is by
standing on the shoulders of Giants.

marginNotes.author: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646--1716)
marginNotes.text:
Music is the pleasure the
human mind experiences from counting without being aware that it is counting.
